symbols: Christ appearing on the altar — the Real Presence in the Eucharist made visible
instruments of the Passion surrounding Christ — the arma Christi as witnesses to the sacrifice
altar with candles and chalice — the liturgical setting of the Mass
Gregory kneeling — the celebrant's faith rewarded with a vision of what the sacrament truly contains
Man of Sorrows posture — Christ's perpetual offering of himself for the sins of the world
description: A tall, narrow panel painting showing Christ as the Man of Sorrows appearing above an altar during Mass. Christ stands in a luminous arched niche, wearing a white loincloth, with instruments of the Passion (crosses, ladder, spear, nails) arranged in the space around him. Below, a priest in white vestments kneels at the altar which holds candles and a chalice. Other clergy and attendants kneel on either side, including a figure in a dark habit and a figure in a red cap. The composition is vertically divided by the altar line, with the miraculous vision above and the earthly Mass below.
theological_description: Bosch's Mass of Saint Gregory depicts the famous medieval legend in which Pope Gregory the Great, while celebrating the Eucharist, was granted a vision of Christ as the Man of Sorrows appearing on the altar — confirming the doctrine of the Real Presence. While the legend itself is post-biblical, it directly illustrates Jesus's own words at the Last Supper: 'This is my body, which is given for you. Do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19). The instruments of the Passion surrounding Christ — the cross, nails, spear, crown of thorns — echo Isaiah's prophecy: 'He was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities' (Isaiah 53:5). The Eucharistic theology visualized here reflects Paul's teaching: 'For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26). The Man of Sorrows posture draws from Isaiah 53:3: 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.' The vision affirms that every celebration of the Eucharist makes present the once-for-all sacrifice of Calvary — as Hebrews teaches, Christ 'entered once for all into the holy places... by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:12).

scripture_refs.reason: The institution of the Eucharist — 'This is my body given for you' — the theological heart of the Mass depicted
Proclaiming the Lord's death in the Eucharist — the Mass as ongoing memorial of the sacrifice
The Man of Sorrows pierced for transgressions — Christ's posture and the surrounding arma Christi
Christ entering the holy place with his own blood — the vision reveals the eternal sacrifice present in the liturgy
Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man — the Eucharistic discourse underlying the Real Presence vision

_meta.title: The Legend of the Mass of St. Gregory
